extracted_facts: Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced a revised target to build 1.5 million new homes across England by 2029.
The target was increased from the previous figure of 1.2 million.
Housing Secretary Angela Rayner confirmed mandatory housing targets for local councils will be reintroduced.
Mandatory targets were made advisory in December 2023 under the previous Conservative government.
The Office for Budget Responsibility estimated in March that 300,000 new homes per year are required to meet the target.
A rate of 300,000 new homes per year has not been achieved in England since 1977.
Shadow Housing Secretary Kemi Badenoch criticized the plan as a press release pretending to be a policy.
The government published a review of the National Planning Policy Framework.
Proposals include releasing grey belt land, defined as previously developed or low-quality greenbelt.
A consultation on the reforms opens on 4 March and runs for eight weeks.
The Local Government Association warned that councils are fundamentally under-resourced to process applications.
The Home Builders Federation stated members could build 250,000 homes per year by 2027 if bottlenecks are removed.
The Home Builders Federation identified a shortage of skilled trades as a limiting factor.
quote_anchors: We inherited a broken housing market, and tinkering at the edges will not fix it. This is about restoring the promise that hard work leads to a home of your own.
a press release pretending to be a policy
headline_options: Starmer Sets 1.5 Million Home Target in Major Planning Reform
Government Reintroduces Mandatory Housing Targets to Tackle England's Shortage
Labour Unveils Grey Belt Housing Plan Amid Local Government Warnings
lede_angle: The UK government has committed to building 1.5 million homes by 2029, reintroducing mandatory local targets to address a long-standing national housing crisis.

sections.angle: Detail the Prime Minister's announcement in Manchester and the specific increase of the housing target to 1.5 million units.
Explain the shift from advisory to mandatory council targets and the historical context of the 300,000-home annual requirement.
Examine the National Planning Policy Framework review and the specific strategy for releasing 'grey belt' land for development.
Present the opposition from Kemi Badenoch regarding overdevelopment and the practical concerns raised by the Local Government Association.
Analyze the industry perspective from the Home Builders Federation regarding capacity constraints and the shortage of skilled trades.
